name: Symmetrical Stepped-Crown Wall Cabinet Unit
description: A large, wide, symmetrical freestanding or wall-placed cabinet unit in light beige painted wood. The unit is substantially wider than tall and is organized into five primary vertical zones from left to right: (1) left outer tower module, (2) left inner open display shelving bay, (3) central storage zone, (4) right inner open display shelving bay, (5) right outer tower module. At the extreme outer left and right edges, narrow decorative trim strips approximately 6cm wide (explicitly client-stated) run the full height of the unit and feature shaped or profiled detailing at their top and bottom ends. The defining top silhouette is stepped and tiered: the outer towers rise to the greatest height, then step down toward the center in a staircase profile — the client's explicit design request. A prominent projecting horizontal ledge or counter surface spans the full width of the central section, separating the upper cabinet and shelving zone from the lower storage zone. UPPER SECTION: Each outer tower contains open shelving in its upper zone (at least one fixed shelf creating two open niches). The two inner bays flanking the center contain open display shelving with multiple compartments of varying heights in a bookcase-style grid arrangement. The central upper zone contains a double-door closed cabinet — two tall doors meeting at a central vertical line, with plain uninterrupted panel faces. LOWER SECTION: Each outer tower contains a single closed door panel in its lower zone. Flanking the central drawer bank on each side is one closed-front base cabinet element, most likely a hinged door. The central lower zone contains six drawers arranged in two columns by three rows, each drawer front bearing a pull mark. The entire unit sits on a continuous full-width plinth base. The finish is light beige painted wood throughout the entire unit with no secondary colors.

dimensions.notes: Overall dimensions not specified by client. The only explicitly client-stated dimension is the outer decorative trim strips at approximately 6cm wide on the far left and far right edges. All other dimensions are unspecified. No reliable scale reference exists in the sketch. Do not invent or assume any measurements.

style.aesthetic: Contemporary transitional with classical stepped-crown millwork character; symmetrical breakfront-inspired composition
influences: Classical breakfront and secretary cabinet tradition
Traditional painted millwork with cornice, plinth block, and pilaster detailing
Symmetrical wall unit and display cabinet systems
Architectural facade composition — client's own reference to 'buildings' for the outer towers

materials.name: MDF or furniture-grade board (painted)
materials.usage: All carcass panels, shelves, door panels, drawer fronts, crown assembly, trim strips, plinth base — entire unit
materials.finish: Light beige — smooth opaque painted finish, single color applied uniformly across the entire unit

materials.name: Back panels — HDF or thin MDF
materials.usage: Rear closure panels for all modules: towers, inner shelving bays, central cabinet
materials.finish: Light beige painted to match front faces — structural requirement inferred, not explicitly stated by client

materials.name: Metal drawer slides
materials.usage: Six drawer runners for the central lower 2×3 drawer bank
materials.finish: Not specified by client — recommend full-extension soft-close slides

materials.name: Door hinges and pulls
materials.usage: All operable door panels: 2 outer tower lower doors + 2 lower flanking base cabinet doors + 2 central upper cabinet doors = 6 doors minimum
materials.finish: Not specified by client — recommend concealed hinges; pulls in champagne, brushed gold, or beige-toned finish to complement painted surface

colors.notes: HIGH confidence. Explicitly stated by client: 'light beiges painted wood'. Single-color painted finish across the entire unit. No secondary colors, contrasting panels, or accent elements specified. Color source is the text brief only — the sketch is a pencil line drawing and provides no color information.

structural_elements.name: Left Outer Decorative Trim Strip
structural_elements.description: Narrow vertical strip at the extreme far-left edge of the unit, approximately 6cm wide (explicitly client-stated dimension). Runs the full height of the unit from plinth top to crown top. Features shaped or profiled decorative detailing at the top end and bottom end — consistent with cap block and plinth block conventions in painted millwork. This is a distinct applied element, not merely the side return of the left tower. Exact profile of the shaped detailing is unresolved — see consensus_notes.
structural_elements.position: Extreme far-left edge, full height of unit

structural_elements.name: Right Outer Decorative Trim Strip
structural_elements.description: Exact mirror image of the left outer decorative trim strip. Approximately 6cm wide, full height, with shaped detailing at top and bottom ends. Exact profile of shaped detailing is unresolved.
structural_elements.position: Extreme far-right edge, full height of unit

structural_elements.name: Left Outer Tower Module
structural_elements.description: Tall vertical cabinet module forming the left boundary of the main unit body, positioned immediately inboard of the left outer trim strip. Rises to the maximum height of the unit — the tallest point of the stepped crown. Organized in two vertical zones: UPPER ZONE — open shelving with at least one fixed horizontal shelf creating two open display niches, fully open front with no doors. LOWER ZONE — single closed door panel (one hinged door). The tower is taller than the inner bays and central section, forming the high outer step of the crown profile.
structural_elements.position: Far left, immediately inboard of left trim strip

structural_elements.name: Right Outer Tower Module
structural_elements.description: Exact bilateral mirror image of the left outer tower module. Same maximum height, same two-zone arrangement: open shelving upper zone with at least one fixed shelf creating two open niches, single closed door panel in lower zone.
structural_elements.position: Far right, immediately inboard of right trim strip

structural_elements.name: Left Inner Open Display Shelving Bay
structural_elements.description: Open-fronted display and bookcase bay positioned between the left outer tower and the central zone. Contains a grid of open compartments created by horizontal and vertical shelf dividers — multiple compartments of varying heights in a bookcase-style arrangement. Fully open front with no doors. The design intent is perfect bilateral symmetry with the right inner bay; any asymmetry visible in the freehand sketch is a drafting artifact and must not be reproduced.
structural_elements.position: Left of center, between left tower and central cabinet zone

structural_elements.name: Right Inner Open Display Shelving Bay
structural_elements.description: Exact bilateral mirror image of the left inner open display shelving bay. Identical compartment grid arrangement — multiple open compartments of varying heights, fully open front.
structural_elements.position: Right of center, between right tower and central cabinet zone

structural_elements.name: Central Upper Double-Door Cabinet
structural_elements.description: Enclosed storage cabinet occupying the upper central zone between the two inner open shelving bays. Features two tall doors of equal width meeting at a central vertical line — a pair of hinged doors opening left and right respectively. Door fronts are plain, uninterrupted panel faces with no internal shelf subdivision lines visible, confirming closed storage behind. The top of this zone is lower than the outer towers, forming the lower step of the stepped crown profile. The top edge of this cabinet carries the shaped cornice molding referenced by the client.
structural_elements.position: Upper center, between left and right inner shelving bays

structural_elements.name: Stepped Crown and Top Profile Assembly
structural_elements.description: The defining top silhouette of the entire unit. The outer towers rise to the maximum height. The crown then steps down in clean horizontal-and-vertical tiers toward the center — the central section top is the lowest tier. Step transitions are clean right-angle geometry, not curved or sloped. A shaped or molded cornice profile is present at the top of the central section per client text. The client explicitly requested this stepped profile 'instead of a plain line.' This is the primary aesthetic signature of the piece and must be executed with precision.
structural_elements.material: Painted MDF/board with applied molding profile at step transitions and cornice
structural_elements.position: Top of entire unit, full width

structural_elements.name: Mid-Level Projecting Ledge
structural_elements.description: A prominent continuous horizontal surface spanning the full width of the central section of the unit (between the two outer towers), separating the upper cabinet and shelving zone from the lower storage zone. Drawn with significant depth and visible underside shading in the sketch, indicating this is a genuinely projecting counter or shelf surface — not merely a structural rail or decorative line. Functions as a natural display surface. Sits at approximately mid-height of the overall unit.
structural_elements.position: Horizontal, spanning full central width at mid-height, between upper and lower sections

structural_elements.name: Lower Left Closed Compartment
structural_elements.description: Closed-front storage element in the lower section, positioned between the left outer tower's lower door and the central drawer bank. Most likely a hinged door base cabinet. The front presents as a plain closed panel. Fixed-panel interpretation remains possible but hinged door is the more functionally logical reading consistent with the overall storage intent.
structural_elements.position: Lower section, left of central drawer bank, right of left tower lower door

structural_elements.name: Lower Right Closed Compartment
structural_elements.description: Exact bilateral mirror image of the lower left closed compartment. Closed-front element, most likely a hinged door base cabinet, positioned between the central drawer bank and the right outer tower's lower door.
structural_elements.position: Lower section, right of central drawer bank, left of right tower lower door

structural_elements.name: Central Lower Drawer Bank
structural_elements.description: Six drawers arranged in two columns by three rows, occupying the central lower zone directly below the mid-level projecting ledge. The two columns are divided by a central vertical partition. Each column contains three drawers stacked vertically. Each drawer front bears a pull mark (tilde or bar mark) at its center. All six drawer fronts are equal in height within their column. The drawer bank is flanked on left and right by the lower closed compartments.
structural_elements.material: Painted MDF/board carcass with metal drawer slides
structural_elements.position: Lower center, directly below mid-level ledge

structural_elements.name: Continuous Plinth Base
structural_elements.description: A full-width continuous plinth or base panel running the entire width of the unit at floor level, supporting all modules. No legs are present — the unit sits directly on this plinth. The plinth has a modest height and projects slightly forward of the carcass face, consistent with traditional millwork base detailing.
structural_elements.position: Bottom, full width of unit, floor level

spatial_layout: A single frontal furniture composition arranged with strict bilateral symmetry around a central vertical axis. Reading from left to right across the full width: [Left outer trim strip ~6cm] — [Left outer tower: open shelving upper / closed door lower] — [Left inner open display shelving bay: multi-compartment bookcase grid] — [Central zone upper: double-door closed cabinet / lower: 2×3 drawer bank flanked by closed compartments] — [Right inner open display shelving bay: mirror of left] — [Right outer tower: mirror of left] — [Right outer trim strip ~6cm]. A projecting mid-level ledge divides the upper and lower sections across the central width. The outer towers are the tallest elements; the crown steps down toward the center. The entire unit rests on a continuous full-width plinth at floor level.

context.placement: Freestanding or wall-placed against an interior wall — likely a living room, reception room, or study feature wall
context.surroundings: Indoor residential or similar interior setting. No architectural enclosure is drawn around the unit in the sketch.
context.scale_reference: No overall dimensions stated by client. The only explicit dimension is the outer trim strips at approximately 6cm wide. Sketch proportions suggest the unit is substantially wider than tall — estimated at roughly 3:1 width-to-height ratio from sketch perspective, but this is approximate and unreliable for fabrication. No human figure or scale marker is present in the sketch.

views_to_generate.label: Floor Plan
views_to_generate.description: Top-down 2D architectural floor plan of the cabinet footprint showing the full width, overall depth, module positions, and outer trim strip returns
views_to_generate.generation_prompt:
Draw a clean, precise 2D architectural floor plan — a top-down view looking straight down at the cabinet as if the ceiling were removed and a camera mounted directly above. This is a flat plan drawing only: no perspective, no shading suggesting height, no 3D elements.

OVERALL FOOTPRINT:
The cabinet is substantially wider than it is deep. The footprint is a simple rectangle — a long horizontal shape. The width is many times greater than the depth. The depth of the unit is uniform across the full width (no projections forward or backward except as noted below).

OUTER BOUNDARY:
Draw the outer boundary of the cabinet as a thick closed rectangle. This represents the outermost walls of the unit as seen from above.

OUTER TRIM STRIPS (LEFT AND RIGHT):
At the extreme left end of the rectangle, draw a thin internal vertical line parallel to the left outer wall, positioned very close to it — approximately one-twentieth of the total width inward from the left edge. This creates a narrow strip at the far left representing the left outer decorative trim element (approximately 6cm wide — the only explicitly stated dimension). Repeat identically at the extreme right end, creating a matching narrow strip at the far right. These two narrow strips are the outermost vertical zones of the plan.

FIVE INTERNAL VERTICAL ZONES:
Between the two outer trim strips, the interior of the footprint is divided into five vertical zones by internal partition lines running front-to-back (parallel to the short sides of the rectangle). From left to right:
— Zone 1 (LEFT OUTER TOWER): A moderately wide zone immediately inboard of the left trim strip. Its depth matches the full cabinet depth.
— Zone 2 (LEFT INNER SHELVING BAY): A wider zone to the right of the left tower. Its depth matches the full cabinet depth.
— Zone 3 (CENTRAL ZONE): The central zone, roughly similar in width to the inner shelving bays. Its depth matches the full cabinet depth.
— Zone 4 (RIGHT INNER SHELVING BAY): Mirror of Zone 2. Identical width to Zone 2.
— Zone 5 (RIGHT OUTER TOWER): Mirror of Zone 1. Identical width to Zone 1.

The five zones are perfectly symmetrical around the centerline of the plan. Zones 1 and 5 are equal. Zones 2 and 4 are equal. Zone 3 is the central zone.

MID-LEVEL LEDGE (PROJECTING SURFACE):
Within the central section (Zones 2, 3, and 4 — between the two outer towers), draw a thin horizontal rectangle slightly inset from the front face of the cabinet, running the full width of those three central zones. This represents the top surface of the projecting mid-level ledge as seen from above. It is a shallow rectangle (narrow front-to-back) sitting at the front of the cabinet in the central section.

PLINTH BASE:
The plinth base runs the full width of the entire unit at floor level. In plan, it may be represented as a very slight forward projection of the front face line at the base — a thin rectangle along the full bottom edge of the footprint, projecting minimally forward of the main carcass front face.

DOORS (shown as door swing arcs in plan):
— LEFT OUTER TOWER LOWER DOOR: In the lower portion of Zone 1, draw a door swing arc. The door is hinged at one side of the zone opening and swings outward (toward the viewer — forward from the front face). Show the door leaf as a thin line and the arc of its swing.
— RIGHT OUTER TOWER LOWER DOOR: Mirror of the above in Zone 5.
— LOWER LEFT CLOSED COMPARTMENT DOOR: In the lower section between Zone 1 and Zone 3, draw a door swing arc opening forward.
— LOWER RIGHT CLOSED COMPARTMENT DOOR: Mirror of the above on the right side.
— CENTRAL UPPER DOUBLE DOORS: In Zone 3, draw two door swing arcs — the left door hinged at the left side of Zone 3 swinging forward-left, and the right door hinged at the right side of Zone 3 swinging forward-right. These are a pair of doors meeting at the center.

OPEN SHELVING BAYS (Zones 2 and 4):
In Zones 2 and 4, draw thin horizontal lines parallel to the front face at regular intervals to represent shelf edges as seen from above. These are open bays — no door swing arcs.

LABELS (if the generation system supports text labels):
Label each zone from left to right: "Left Trim," "Left Tower," "Left Shelving Bay," "Central Zone," "Right Shelving Bay," "Right Tower," "Right Trim."
Label the mid-level ledge: "Projecting Ledge."
Label the plinth: "Plinth Base."

STYLE: Clean architectural line drawing. Black lines on white background. Thick lines for outer walls, medium lines for internal partitions, thin lines for shelves and interior details. Door swing arcs as thin curved lines. No color fill. No hatching. No dimensions except the label "~6cm" pointing to the left and right trim strips if text is supported.

views_to_generate.label: Front Elevation — Full Cabinet Face
views_to_generate.description: Frontal 2D elevation of the complete symmetrical cabinet showing the stepped top profile, all five vertical zones, all doors, drawers, open shelving compartments, mid-level ledge, outer trim strips, and plinth base
views_to_generate.generation_prompt:
Draw a clean, precise 2D architectural front elevation — a flat orthographic view looking straight at the front face of the cabinet. No perspective, no foreshortening, no depth cues, no shadows suggesting 3D form. This is a flat drawing of the front face only.

OVERALL PROPORTIONS:
The cabinet is substantially wider than it is tall — approximately three times as wide as it is tall (this is an approximate proportion from the sketch, not a confirmed measurement). The overall outline is a wide horizontal rectangle, but the top edge is NOT a straight horizontal line — it is a stepped profile as described below.

THE STEPPED TOP PROFILE (most critical element):
The top silhouette of the cabinet is a staircase shape stepping down from the outer edges toward the center. Describe it from left to right:
— The extreme left outer trim strip rises to the full maximum height of the unit.
— The left outer tower rises to the same maximum height as the trim strip — this is the tallest point.
— Moving inward, the top steps DOWN to a lower level at the left inner shelving bay. This step is a clean right-angle drop — a short vertical line down, then a horizontal line inward.
— Moving further inward, the top steps DOWN again to the lowest level at the central zone. Another clean right-angle step.
— The central zone top is the lowest point of the stepped crown.
— The right side mirrors the left exactly: the right inner shelving bay top is at the same intermediate height as the left, and the right outer tower and right trim strip rise back up to the maximum height.
— The result is a symmetrical staircase silhouette: tall outer edges, intermediate middle steps, lowest center — like a wide shallow W-shape or a classical stepped pediment.
— At the top of the central zone, there is a shaped cornice molding — render this as a simple projecting horizontal band with a slight profile (a stepped or ogee-like cap) at the top edge of the central cabinet.

OUTER TRIM STRIPS (leftmost and rightmost elements):
At the extreme left and right edges of the elevation, draw narrow vertical strips running the full height of the unit. These strips are approximately one-twentieth of the total width each (the only confirmed dimension is ~6cm — render them as visibly narrow but distinct). At the very top of each strip, draw a small shaped block accent (a simple square or slightly profiled cap block). At the very bottom of each strip, draw a small shaped block accent (a simple square plinth block). These shaped accents give the strips their decorative character.

FIVE VERTICAL ZONES (reading left to right across the full width):
The front face is divided into five main vertical zones between the two outer trim strips. The zones are perfectly symmetrical around the vertical centerline.

ZONE 1 — LEFT OUTER TOWER (far left, tallest):
This zone runs from the plinth base at the bottom to the maximum height at the top.
— UPPER PORTION (approximately the upper two-thirds of the tower height): Open shelving. Draw one horizontal shelf line dividing this upper portion into two open rectangular niches stacked vertically. The niches are open — no door panels, no hardware. The back wall of each niche is visible as a plain rectangle.
— LOWER PORTION (approximately the lower one-third of the tower height): A single closed door panel. Draw a plain rectangular door panel filling this lower zone. The door has a small centered pull handle (a simple horizontal bar or knob) at approximately mid-height of the door panel, positioned toward the inner edge of the door (where it would be reached from the front).
— A horizontal dividing line separates the upper open shelving zone from the lower door zone.

ZONE 2 — LEFT INNER OPEN DISPLAY SHELVING BAY:
This zone is wider than the tower and shorter — its top is at the intermediate step height (lower than the tower, higher than the center). It runs from the mid-level projecting ledge at the bottom up to its stepped top.
— This entire zone is open shelving — no doors.
— Inside, draw a grid of open rectangular compartments created by horizontal shelves and one or two vertical dividers. The compartments vary in height — some taller, some shorter — creating a bookcase-like staggered grid. There should be approximately six to nine visible open compartments arranged in a roughly two-column, three-to-four-row grid, with compartments of varying heights. The exact arrangement should look like a well-organized bookcase display bay.
— No hardware. No door panels. The back wall of each compartment is visible.

ZONE 3 — CENTRAL ZONE (center, lowest top):
This zone is divided horizontally into an UPPER section and a LOWER section by the mid-level projecting ledge.

CENTRAL UPPER SECTION (above the mid-level ledge):
— A double-door closed cabinet. Draw two tall rectangular door panels of equal width side by side, meeting at a central vertical line at the exact centerline of the unit. The doors fill the full width of Zone 3 and the full height from the ledge top up to the central stepped crown top.
— The door panels are plain and uninterrupted — no visible shelf lines, no glass, no decorative molding on the door faces. Each door has a small pull handle positioned near the central meeting edge (left door handle near its right edge; right door handle near its left edge).
— At the very top of this central cabinet, the shaped cornice molding projects slightly above the door tops.

CENTRAL LOWER SECTION (below the mid-level ledge):
— Six drawers arranged in two columns by three rows. The two columns are divided by a central vertical partition line at the exact centerline of the unit. Each column contains three drawers of equal height stacked vertically. All six drawer fronts are plain rectangular panels. Each drawer front has a small centered pull mark (a short horizontal bar or tilde mark) at its horizontal center. The six drawers fill the full width of Zone 3 from the plinth top up to the underside of the mid-level ledge.

MID-LEVEL PROJECTING LEDGE:
A continuous horizontal band spanning the full width of Zones 2, 3, and 4 (between the two outer towers). This ledge is drawn as a horizontal rectangle with visible thickness — it projects forward of the cabinet face. Show the top surface as a horizontal line and the front face of the ledge as a slightly deeper horizontal band below it, with a visible underside line. This is a genuine projecting shelf surface, not a flat decorative line.

ZONE 4 — RIGHT INNER OPEN DISPLAY SHELVING BAY:
Exact bilateral mirror image of Zone 2. Identical open shelving compartment grid, identical proportions, identical height.

ZONE 5 — RIGHT OUTER TOWER (far right, tallest):
Exact bilateral mirror image of Zone 1. Same maximum height, same two-zone arrangement: open shelving upper (two niches divided by one shelf), single closed door panel lower with centered pull handle.

LOWER SECTION — FLANKING CLOSED COMPARTMENTS:
Between the left outer tower's lower door (Zone 1 lower) and the central drawer bank (Zone 3 lower), draw one closed door panel filling that lower zone. This is the lower left closed compartment. It has a small centered pull handle. Mirror this exactly on the right side between the central drawer bank and the right outer tower's lower door. These two flanking compartment doors sit at the same height as the central drawer bank — from plinth top to the underside of the mid-level ledge.

PLINTH BASE:
A continuous horizontal band running the full width of the entire unit at the very bottom. The plinth has a modest height — approximately one-tenth of the total unit height. It projects very slightly forward of the main carcass face. Draw it as a plain horizontal rectangle. A ground line sits below the plinth.

SYMMETRY CHECK:
The entire elevation must be perfectly symmetrical around the vertical centerline. The centerline passes through: the center of the central double-door cabinet (the meeting line of the two doors), the center of the 2×3 drawer bank (the vertical partition between the two drawer columns), and the center of the plinth base.

STYLE: Clean architectural line drawing. Black lines on white background. Thick lines for the outer silhouette and major zone divisions. Medium lines for door and drawer panel outlines. Thin lines for shelf lines, compartment dividers, and hardware marks. No color fill. No hatching. No shading. No perspective. The stepped top silhouette must be drawn with crisp right-angle corners — no curves or slopes at the step transitions. If dimension annotations are added by the generation system, the only confirmed value is "~6cm" for the outer trim strip width.

generation_notes: CRITICAL INSTRUCTIONS FOR ALL VIEWS: (1) SYMMETRY IS ABSOLUTE — every element on the left must mirror exactly on the right. Do not reproduce any asymmetries from the freehand sketch. (2) THE STEPPED CROWN IS THE PRIMARY DESIGN FEATURE — the outer towers must be visibly and significantly taller than the center. The step transitions are clean right-angle geometry. This silhouette must read clearly and dramatically. (3) THE OUTER TRIM STRIPS ARE ~6CM WIDE — this is the only explicitly client-stated dimension. Render them as narrow but distinct vertical elements at the extreme left and right edges, with shaped/profiled detailing at their top and bottom ends. Do not omit them or merge them into the tower sides. (4) FINISH IS UNIFORM LIGHT BEIGE PAINTED WOOD — no wood grain visible, no contrasting colors, no glass panels, no metal accents on the carcass. (5) SIX DRAWERS IN 2 COLUMNS × 3 ROWS — not three full-width drawers. The central vertical division between the two drawer columns must be visible. (6) CENTRAL UPPER ZONE IS A DOUBLE-DOOR CLOSED CABINET — two plain tall door panels meeting at center. Do NOT show open shelving in this zone. (7) TOWER CONFIGURATION IS TWO-ZONE ONLY — open shelving upper, single closed door lower. Do NOT add a third zone or a top door above the open shelving (Gemini's three-zone reading was rejected by majority). (8) DO NOT ADD four small upper doors spanning the top of the central section — this element was proposed by Gemini only and rejected by Claude and GPT as unconfirmed. (9) THE MID-LEVEL LEDGE IS A PROJECTING SURFACE — it has visible depth and an underside. Render it as a genuine counter/shelf projection, not a flat line. (10) PLINTH BASE IS CONTINUOUS FULL WIDTH — no legs. (11) UNRESOLVED — OUTER TRIM STRIP PROFILE: The exact carved or molded profile of the shaped detailing at the top and bottom of the outer trim strips is not confirmed. Render as simple but distinct geometric block accents (square cap block at top, square plinth block at bottom) unless client provides further clarification. (12) UNRESOLVED — LOWER FLANKING COMPARTMENTS: Render as hinged door panels (the more functionally logical interpretation) but note to client that confirmation is needed. (13) NO DIMENSIONS should appear on the floor plan or elevation unless the generation system requires them — if dimension lines are added, the only confirmed value is ~6cm for the outer trim strip width.
